fragments.title: 1913: On the Detection, Analysis, and Estimation of Gases Occluded in Rocks and Minerals
fragments.description:
Author: Dr. Thomas R. Edelstein  
Abstract: Edelstein developed an improved procedure for the detection and estimation of gases evolved from geological samples, with a specific focus on hydrocarbons. His technique greatly enhanced the commonly used methods of fractional distillation and spectrometric analysis, enabling more accurate identification of light hydrocarbons such as methane. He applied the method with success to terrestrial coal-bed samples and suggested that it might prove useful in broader geochemical applications. (E.P.R.)

fragments.title: 1929: Observations on the Anomalous Detection of Methane in the Free Atmosphere
fragments.description:
Author: Prof. Edith L. Bywater  
Abstract: Bywater built upon Edelstein's earlier methods for detecting hydrocarbons in order to investigate the isotopic signatures of methane in diverse atmospheric environments. Though her study originally aimed to understand the atmospheric effects of industrial emissions, her research uncovered significant isotopic variations in regions with swampy soils and stagnant waters. These anomalies suggested natural processes of methane release into the atmosphere that warranted further study, especially in unexplored geographies. (E.P.R.)

fragments.title: 1938: The Development and Construction of a Deep-Sea Apparatus for Ocean Floor Observation
fragments.description:
Author: Dr. Henrik K. Fjordsen  
Abstract: Fjordsen described the development of a deep-sea diving apparatus capable of withstanding pressures corresponding to ocean depths of 4,000 meters. Designed to enable direct human observation of the deep-sea ocean floor, the apparatus comprised a reinforced air chamber constructed of high-strength steel; fused-quartz windows; internal supplies of compressed oxygen; chemical trays to absorb carbon dioxide and moisture; and internal fans to circulate the air. The whole was suspended by a steel cable from a surface vessel. His record-breaking preliminary dives took place near the continental shelf, during which unexpected gaseous emissions of considerable magnitude were observed rising from disturbed sediment, though further investigation was limited by physical and material constraints. (E.P.R.)

fragments.title: 1947: Thermal Gradients and Gas Dynamics in Deep-Sea Ocean Floor Sediments
fragments.description:
Author: Dr. Margaret H. Sinclair  
Abstract: Sinclair made use of the advanced diving apparatus designed by Dr. Henrik K. Fjordsen to explore thermal gradients in deep-sea sediments. Her analysis revealed vast oceanic expanses of unexpected warmth correlated with violent discharges of gas on an extensive scale near fault lines. Though initially interpreted as geological anomalies, she theorized that the emissions were the result of active subsurface processes influencing marine sediment dynamics. She concluded that a definitive determination remained, as yet, beyond the scope of her research. (E.P.R.)

fragments.title: 1949: Stability Thresholds of Gas-Bearing Sediments Under Environmental Stress
fragments.description:
Author: Dr. Elias P. Rowan  
Abstract: As part of our broader research effort, I abstracted dozens of foundational papers dating back to the 1910s that underpin our current work. Now, for the first time, this project synthesizes the implications of those earlier studies. Our research investigated the stability of methane-hydrate formations under controlled thermal and pressure variations. Using piston-core samples recovered by Fjordsen and Sinclair, our experiments provided strong evidence that slight increases in temperature and modest shifts in hydrostatic pressure could lead to rapid dissociation of methane hydrates and the liberation of methane gas. The potential for a chain reaction of methane emission across the ocean floor was discussed, with implications for both natural systems and human activities in submarine regions. As a matter of urgency, I recommended further investigation and monitoring of the sea floor to provide a more comprehensive assessment of these phenomena.

fragments.title: Personal Notes – Dr. Elias P. Rowan
fragments.description:
Archived Documents – Unpublished Letters and Logs
Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution
September 12, 1950
A white seagull glides past my window, catching gusts of wind and hovering over the sparkling sands without a care in the world. It isn't aware of its eventual fate. "Soar, little bird," I want to tell it. "Stay up there forever." The world soon won't offer it any rest. What use is flying if you must eventually land?

My predictive models work flawlessly. My observations are undeniable. Vast methane deposits locked for millennia beneath the ocean floor are destabilizing and escaping into the atmosphere. The methane will drive the warming of the Earth, flood every continent, and poison the very air we breathe... and not in the far future, but in the coming decades. It’s no longer theory or speculation. It's happening. Every new data run, from every ocean basin on Earth, only reinforces the verdict. The cascade has begun. Is there any way to reverse it?

Humanity built its shining towers on a foundation of sand and borrowed time, oblivious to the rising tide already lapping at our ankles. The people in power know now. They refuse to release any information to the public. Instead, they quietly establish black budgets to fund secret projects. They plan to build vast fleets of ships and rockets. They're not even trying to prevent the global catastrophe. They only intend to save themselves and their families.

I've gathered my entire body of scientific evidence and sent it to Evelyn James. She's the only person I trust to understand the full scope of what's about to happen and figure out what must be done. If anyone can chart a path forward for humanity, it's her. By myself, I can do nothing more.

fragments.title: November 3, 1950
fragments.description:
Evelyn called today. She immediately grasped the dire implications of my scientific conclusions. She didn't try to deny them or argue alternative theories. Instead, Evelyn spoke with the calm, terrible resolve of someone who had long suspected the truth, but now held enough proof to act.

She asked sharp and focused questions, particularly about timeframes and thresholds. Incredibly, she already had the outlines of a plan. We stayed on the line for hours, through the night and into the gray light of early dawn.

Before we hung up, Evelyn warned me, "Everyone will call us crazy, Elias. But future generations, if we succeed in giving them a future, will name us prophets."

I pray she’s right.

fragments.title: January 29, 1951
fragments.description:
My warnings are too inconvenient. The institute is quietly sidelining my research. They're reassigning me to administrative busywork. My funding is gone, and my office is now a dusty, windowless closet at the far end of the hall.

Meanwhile, Evelyn presses forward with her plans. Each letter from her becomes more purposeful and determined than the last. She dreams of orbital colonies, modular habitats drifting in the void of space. She calls her project "MOSES." Her plan is nothing less than an exodus from the Earth itself, a lifeboat for whatever sliver of humanity we manage to save.

fragments.title: July 8, 1951
fragments.description:
Evelyn called at the crack of dawn. She seemed exhausted, but triumphant.

"Clear out your desk and pack your bags, Elias. MOSES received the green light!" she announced. I stood there in shock; I don't remember exactly what I said in response. I expressed doubts.

"The world won't understand what we're trying to do, not for a long time, perhaps not in our lifetime. But one day, it will," she replied with new conviction. "When future generations look down upon the Earth from the stars, they'll remember the people who saved them before they were even born."

Her words lodged somewhere deep inside me. Still stunned, I walked the beaches around the institute one last time. I haven't seen the seagull for many months now. Maybe it senses the rising tide, slow and relentless? Maybe it left in search of higher, safer ground? Wherever it has gone, the brave bird will fly until its wings give out. Likewise, humanity will endure... until we cannot any longer.

But maybe... just maybe... we have somewhere else to go now. Thank you, Evelyn. It's nice to feel hope again.

fragments.title: From: Executive Office of the President
fragments.description:
Office of Science and Technology Policy – Coordination Directive
To: All Relevant Agencies
Request ID: 005423/A/1956
Subject: Mandatory Requirements for All Public-Facing Climate and Sea Level Communications
Details: Effective immediately, all draft publications, reports, fact sheets, or public communications from relevant agencies that reference projections for long-term sea-level rise, multidecadal climate modeling, or related climate change studies must be submitted for OMB clearance (in coordination with OSTP and the White House Office of Communications) prior to publication or release.

The focus of all materials submitted for approval must shift toward local mitigation efforts, community recovery efforts, and advancements in near-term weather forecasting.

[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Enforce an optimistic tone and 'progress for all' narratives. Flag all deviations for director-level review."

fragments.title: Budget Reprogramming Directive
fragments.description:
From: Office of Management and Budget
To: National Science Foundation
Office of Naval Research, Department of the Navy
Bureau of Commercial Fisheries, Department of the Interior
United States Coast and Geodetic Survey
[and all other agencies holding relevant marine research funds]

Request ID: 010732/B/1956
Subject: Reallocation of Oceanic and Marine Research Funding
Details: Pursuant to current Administration priorities, OMB authority, and classified National Security Council Directive NSC 5612 (Top Secret), effective immediately, 90% of competitively awarded grants and cooperative agreements in marine biology, oceanography, and deep-sea exploration are to be reprogrammed and redirected toward space-based observation of the sea, oceanic atmospheric studies, and sea-to-orbit logistics and infrastructure.

All affected projects shall be reclassified under the umbrella designation "MOSES" and linked to interagency project ID: MOSES-56-ESC, to be managed by NASA's newly proposed Earth Science Division in coordination with OMB.

[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Do NOT mention any breakdown of specific allocations in public reports."

fragments.title: Public Affairs Guidance
fragments.description:
From: Office of the Assistant Secretary of Defense for Public Affairs
To: All DoD Public Affairs Officers
Associated Contractors and Local Officials (as briefed)

Request ID: 020981/C/1956
Subject: Media Engagement Regarding Sensitive Rural Construction
Details: Effective immediately, DoD personnel, contractors, and associated local officials shall not confirm, discuss, or provide any information to media regarding any rural construction activities that require Top Secret or higher clearance. All media inquiries must be referred to OASD(PA).

Press releases identifying the facilities as “high-yield agricultural labs” will be distributed for official use.

[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Control narrative at all costs. Coordination with Defense PR is MANDATORY."

fragments.title: Research Coordination Directive
fragments.description:
From: Office of the Secretary of Defense
To:   Office of the Assistant Secretary of Defense (Research and Development)

Request ID: 045812/D/1956
Subject: Commissioning of Psychological Impact Studies on Controlled Populations
Details: The Assistant Secretary of Defense (Research and Development) is hereby authorized to commission classified studies to evaluate public psychological and behavioral responses to hypothetical large-scale migration and evacuation scenarios. Studies shall draw focus groups only from select urban centers or isolated rural communities. All research must be conducted under academic or contractor cover. Results will directly inform long-term population management and contingency strategies of the U.S. federal government.

[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Scenarios should be proposed as unrelated 'hypothetical models' at the various universities."

fragments.title: Security Enhancement Directive
fragments.description:
From: Office of the Secretary of Defense
To: Special Security Office – Internal Coordination

Request ID: 050129/E/1956
Subject: Revision of Classified Information Protocols
Details: Effective immediately, all civilian contractors, consultants, and personnel associated with Project MOSES-56-ESC are required to sign revised Non-Disclosure Agreements incorporating express acknowledgment that unauthorized disclosure or mishandling of project information constitutes a violation punishable under the Espionage Act of 1917 and related statutes (18 U.S.C. § 794). Furthermore, all offices, facilities, and contractor sites that handle MOSES-56-ESC classified material shall implement enhanced internal monitoring standards, including tightened personnel security procedures, stricter logging of access, and full implementation of internal surveillance as required by Executive Order 10501 and applicable service security directives.

[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Office memo shredders to be replaced with incinerators by Q4."

fragments.title: Personnel Acquisition Directive
fragments.description:
From: Office of the Secretary of Defense
To: Office of Special Projects – Internal Coordination

Request ID: 060943/F/1956
Subject: Recruitment Campaign for Specialized Workforce
Details: You are authorized to immediately initiate discreet recruitment of preeminent engineers, biologists, and physicists for Project MOSES-56-ESC. Select universities and leading civilian research facilities constitute initial recruitment targets. All position announcements, correspondence, and discussions must frame these positions as essential for "national defense" or "advanced infrastructure development." No unclassified references to project specifics are authorized. All recruitment activities must remain in compliance with Request ID: 050129/E/1956.

[Handwritten Note in Margin]: "Avoid using any language that describes 'outer space' or 'migration' during recruitment."

data.title: MOSES Directives
data.description: Directives to Ensure Long-Term Human Continuity

fragments.title: Log Entry 1962: "The First Mandate"
fragments.description:
Subject: MOSES Core Statement of Intent
Author: Dr. Elias Carver, Chief Strategic Officer
Location: MOSES Initiative Headquarters
"The global situation could not be clearer. Earth’s prognosis is terminal. We cannot save the planet, but we might be able to save some of its people. Our sole priority must be ensuring the continuity of humanity. All MOSES directives, resources, decisions, and actions derive their legitimacy from this objective. No other consideration may take precedence. Our goal: the construction of a network of orbital stations capable of housing humanity on a permanent basis across generations. Everything else is secondary: art, culture, even individual lives. Our mission objective is survival."

Notes:
Reprioritize the MOSES project toward the development of modular orbital station designs and closed-loop, multi-generational habitation systems.
Address the emerging ethical debates around our prioritization of select populations for initial evacuation.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1968: "The Science Imperative"
fragments.description:
Subject: Allocating Resources to Scientific Advancement
Author: Dr. Miriam Goldstein, Director of Research
Location: MOSES R&D Facility
"Without constant innovation, survival is impossible. To thrive, we must accelerate breakthroughs in energy, life support, and materials engineering. Scientific research is not a luxury; it is our lifeline."

Notes:
Countered objections from early stakeholders who consider scientific funding a diversion of resources from urgent, immediate needs.
Reviewed prototype designs for cryogenic storage of humans and for Caretaker robots.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1973: "Sustainability or Extinction"
fragments.description:
Subject: Developing Efficient Closed-Loop Resource Management Strategies
Author: Dr. Katherine Leung, Chief Ecologist
Location: New Terra Planning Summit
"In space, waste equals death. Every gram of matter must serve a purpose. It must enter a cycle and find a second life, then a third and a fourth, for as long as physically possible. Our design goals must extend beyond the mere use and reuse of precious resources. Our aspirational ideal, however unreachable, is to preserve every drop of water and every molecule of air in an infinite cycle of reuse."

Notes:
Suggested revisions and improvements to existing closed-loop life support systems in orbital station designs.
Debates arose over balancing resource efficiency with human comfort.
Sustainability metrics proposed for usage of resources across all orbital colonies.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1980: "Order Over Chaos"
fragments.description:
Subject: Ensuring Social Cohesion in Orbital Colonies
Author: Admiral Victor Kruger, Security Advisor
Location: MOSES Leadership Council
"Humans are not machines! Without stability, they will rebel, fracture, and tear down everything we build. Social cohesion is not an option; it’s a necessity. Our margin for survival is extremely narrow. Therefore, unity must be enforced, even at the expense of individual freedom."

Notes:
Discussions on restricting individual freedoms to ensure collective survival.
Early drafts of colony law include severe penalties for "unreasonable" dissent.
Concerns raised about the long-term psychological effects of authoritarian governance.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1986: "The Council's Burden"
fragments.description:
Subject: Establishing Transparency in Proposed Government Structures
Author: Dr. James Thatcher, Ethics Advisor
Location: MOSES Ethics Committee Meeting
"Power must have limits. The Council must serve humanity, not the other way around. Checks and balances are not a weakness. They are a vital safeguard against tyranny."

Notes:
Discussed initial plans for Council elections and term limits.
Countered opposition from military advisors who argue transparency will compromise security.
Agreed to record all future Council decisions for archival purposes.

fragments.title: Log Entry 1992: "The Doctrine Framework"
fragments.description:
Subject: Converting Doctrine into Actionable Policies
Author: Dr. Helena Vasquez, Policy Architect
Location: New Terra Governance Complex
"We must thoroughly understand the necessary concepts: survival, science, sustainability, order, and accountability. These are the pillars upon which humanity’s future rests. What remains is the hardest part: transforming our ideals into reality."

Notes:
Circulated final drafts of New Terra's doctrinal framework for review and approval.
Debates over balancing efficiency versus fairness in resource allocation.
Decision made to delay public rollout due to growing unrest on Earth.

fragments.title: Log Entry 2000: "The Earth Legacy Debate"
fragments.description:
Subject: Reevaluating Doctrine in Light of Earth’s Collapse
Author: Dr. Samuel Nyarko, Historian
Location: Decaying Earth Advisory Council
"Our founding doctrine assumes a future where Earth still plays a role in humanity’s destiny. That belief has proven sentimental. Earth is a dying relic now and will eventually become entirely uninhabitable and outside our reach. How can we meaningfully preserve its legacy while fully committing our precious resources to survival among the stars?"

Notes:
Proposals to archive Earth’s cultural and historical artifacts in orbital museums.
Debates on whether Earth-bound populations should continue receiving resources.
Discussions regarding the rising tensions between colony inhabitants and remaining Earth governments.

fragments.title: August 1, 1980
fragments.description: "Enjoyed morning tea on the balcony today. All quiet, except for the hum of the generators. The tide maps show another half-meter rise since last month. For what it's worth, we reported it to the world but it's pretty obvious we're running out of time. The design team worked through the night again. Our discussions about the colony modules feels... lighter today. Progress, perhaps?"

fragments.title: March 15, 1982
fragments.description: "The jasmine in my garden bloomed early this year. Funny, isn’t it? Nature finds a way even when we’re... well, planning to leave. The comms from Geneva finally gave us a clear commitment today: they approved Phase Two. I should feel relief, but instead I feel uneasy in my own garden, like every plant suddenly feels borrowed."

fragments.title: December 4, 1985
fragments.description: "I feel silly trying to bake a cake when so many ingredients are being rationed. Still, Henry loved it. Everyone could use a few small delights in their lives. Another shuttle launch is scheduled for tomorrow. They've become routine now, yet each one feels heavier than the last. I wonder if our hopes and fears are weighing those rockets down."

fragments.title: June 22, 1988
fragments.description: "Rain keeps falling. It's been falling nonstop for weeks. Roads have become rivers; rivers are now lakes. The authorities relocated the evacuation zones further inland again. I barely recognize maps anymore. Tonight, I heard laughter from the control room. It echoed in the emptiness. It was nice to feel some warmth in a place that’s felt cold for so long."

fragments.title: January 1, 1990
fragments.description: "Henry found an old vinyl record player in our storage room. We danced to something slow, nostalgic, and scratchy. For a brief moment, our world felt timeless. The damn budget discussions are dragging on again. They refuse to see what I see. Or maybe they do, and that’s what scares them."

fragments.title: September 3, 1991
fragments.description: "They’re calling humanity's move into orbit the ‘Exodus.’ The word feels too grand, too Biblical. We're just moving people so they can survive. That's the whole plan. Yet I can’t help but think of the ancient story about Noah and his Ark. Except our ark doesn't grant a fresh start after a deluge. It doesn't carry you to salvation or any promised land. Humanity will remain trapped aboard for God knows how many generations. It's a boat forever tottering at the edge of an endless whirlpool.

fragments.title: April 18, 1993
fragments.description: "Our generator failed again last night. Thankfully, the backup kicked in, but it rattled so loudly that I could barely sleep. The preliminary status reports from the colonies are... suspiciously optimistic. Maybe they feel a need to put a positive spin on everything. That kind of optimism is just another form of denial. It won’t help their chance for survival."

fragments.title: July 7, 1995
fragments.description: "I tried to plant basil in some of the old pots today. It didn’t take. Nothing seems to take anymore, at least not down here on Earth. The doctors say I need rest, but I can’t afford to stop. There’s still so much left to fix. The team doesn’t know about my medical condition yet. I’ll tell them soon. Maybe."

fragments.title: October 14, 1995
fragments.description: "The nights grow frigid. I sit by the console all day long, watching the data streams roll in. I find comfort in the numbers, even when they’re grim. The colonies sent an amazing message today. Just three words: ‘We are growing.’ That’s all I needed to hear. That feels like enough."

fragments.title: February 2, 1996
fragments.description: "Diagnosis day. Medical scans confirmed that I have pancreatic cancer, stage four. When the doctor told me, I felt numb, not shocked. Maybe my body already knew. Henry’s eyes went hollow when I told him. For months, we'd both suspected something was wrong with me. We have so little hope to cling to anymore, here or anywhere. Despite everything, I refuse to stop working. The colonies need me. Earth needs me. I must see our project through."

fragments.title: April 17, 1996
fragments.description: "My hands tremble now; pens and pencils feel heavier every day. I think the cancer treatments are consuming as much of my remaining life as the disease itself. But the daily reports keep transmitting in from the orbital colonies. They speak of crops harvested, children born, and progress made. It's comforting, but I feel increasingly distant and isolated, like a ghost guiding humanity’s course into the future. I always imagined myself up there when the colonies finally began to flourish, when they finally got all their critical systems up and running. I thought I'd witness the culmination of my dreams, but every dreamer has to wake up before the dawn."

fragments.title: September 9, 1996
fragments.description: "Henry brought me fresh jasmine today. Its scent carried me back years, to those fleeting moments on our balcony, back when tides were still manageable. Now the oceans have consumed entire countries; the final evacuation ships are scheduled to launch within weeks. My energy dwindles rapidly now. Every day feels shorter than the last; every breath shallower than the previous one. My mind keeps wandering to those simple joys: morning tea, baking cakes, and the laughter of colleagues. Life is so beautiful, and so stubborn in its persistence. Why have I realized this only now?"

fragments.title: December 28, 1996
fragments.description: "Christmas passed quietly. I recorded my farewell to the orbital colonies yesterday. I barely finished through my tears, but I had to deliver it: a message of hope, guidance, and sorrow all woven together. Afterward, the room felt colder and more silent than ever, except for the never-ending hum of the generators. Henry remained at my side and held my hand. We barely spoke. Words felt insufficient. Time was short, and the list of our regrets... endless. Have I done enough? Did I prepare humanity well enough? The answers refused to come."

fragments.title: January 14, 1997
fragments.description: "Today was a struggle. My vision is blurry now; writing is nearly impossible. Henry takes up the pen when I can no longer hold it. My life's purpose feels distant and hazy. The sky is always gray; the floods outside are relentless. But now the shuttles are launching from Earth. The Exodus has begun. Communication with the colonies is becoming unreliable and spotty. We might eventually be completely cut off. Am I abandoning them? Or am I the one being abandoned? Ah, so much uncertainty. Sleep comes sporadically now. My fragmented dreams are always full of oceans and falling stars."

fragments.title: February 2, 1997 (Henry)
fragments.description: "Evelyn asked me to write this entry for her. Her strength is failing fast, and she can barely speak. But her eyes still carry their fire. She’s proud, afraid, hopeful, and heartbroken. She feels every emotion all at once. Ensuring humanity’s survival was her life’s work. She carried the burden for so long. We all carry it. We can't drop it, no matter the cost. To whoever reads this, please find a way to carry that burden onward."

fragments.title: February 7, 1997 (Henry)
fragments.description: "She’s gone. Evelyn passed quietly in her sleep just before dawn. Her room was peaceful. Her final breath was gentle, like a whispered goodbye. Outside, the ocean continues to rise. The world mourns her, at least what’s left of it. The rest lies quietly beneath endless water. Without her, I feel more lost and alone than ever. She believed in humanity. She believed in you, dear unknown reader. Honor her legacy, I beg you."

fragments.title: Final Note – Discovered by the Last Remaining Humans (December 14, 2091)
fragments.description:
"We found her journals today, buried beneath debris in what used to be Mission Control for the Exodus. The name of Evelyn James still echoes in these crumbling corridors. It's tragic; most pages are faded or destroyed by water. But this last note was sealed with great care. Perhaps she knew someone might find her one day.

Nothing exists outside this abandoned facility except the endless ocean and the drowned ruins of the old world. She was a prophet. Thanks to her, some of humanity escaped Earth’s watery grave. Many of us were left behind, though. We’re just survivors scattered and adrift in a flooded world, trying to make sense of it all. Thanks to her meticulous records, we now know the truth: this world was always destined to drown.

She gave everything to save humanity: her health, her happiness, even her very life. Reading her words, I can feel her doubt: a quiet anguish that maybe humanity wasn’t worth saving. The skies are silent and empty now. We can only hope the colony she dreamed of still thrives somewhere beyond the clouds, beyond our reach.

Her burden would crush us. We are a ragtag band of scavengers, desperately searching for scraps. All we can do is store these journals in a secure place and pray someone stronger finds them, someone who can carry what we cannot.

Forgive us, Dr. James. We remembered you too late.

Goodbye, journal. We close you now with reverence, as one might close the eyes of the dead."

fragments.title: Event 1: First Glance
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]  
Today, I spotted a sweet, innocent moment between a young man and a young woman in one of the quiet corners of New Terra. They like each other but are still too shy to admit it openly. He's a tech apprentice responsible for maintenance in the transit corridor. She works as a farmer in Hydroponics Bay 3. They both pretended to admire the gardens of the central atrium, but they kept stealing shy glances at each other. Somehow, they “randomly” ended up together beside the blooming jasmine vine we’d cultivated to boost station morale. The young man finally found the courage to strike up a conversation. Ah, what a pleasant surprise to witness the first delicate blossoming of young love, still unburdened by the heavy austerity of orbital life.

fragments.title: Event 2: Love in Bloom
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]  
The lovers are inseparable now. She brings him nutrient capsules during his shifts, and he knows her work schedule by heart. Whenever she tends the plants, he finds ways to help... anything to spend a few more precious minutes with her. I overheard them planning a romantic picnic under the artificial oak grove on Park Deck 2. Love still blooms up here in New Terra, despite all our resource restrictions and unforgiving work schedules. I almost feel guilty being a secret witness to something so pure.

fragments.title: Event 3: A New Beginning
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]  
Today, they officially registered as partners. They stood side-by-side, holding hands, as they signed their mutual consent forms at the colony’s registry desk. Their marriage ceremony is scheduled to take place during the next artificial sunrise. Nothing extravagant is planned: just close friends, recycled decorations, and the fragile hope of building a new life together. I felt a pang of envy as I processed their request for shared quarters. Wandering the endless corridors of New Terra can feel quite lonely without love like theirs.

fragments.title: Event 4: A Cry of New Life
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]  
The colony buzzes with life today. The married couple welcomed their first child, a tiny bundle of perfection named Arin. Arin’s cries echo loudly down the station halls. It's a sound we rarely get to hear anymore in orbit. I delivered their extra rations myself so I could catch another glimpse of their little miracle. The new parents seemed exhausted but radiant. In a world far removed from Earth, a newborn's cry is a victory against the cold, heartless void.

fragments.title: Event 5: Shadows of Strain
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]  
Parenthood is never easy, especially under New Terra’s relentless work regimen. I see their smiles fade as the sleepless nights and ration shortages wear them down. Their laughter has grown softer; their arguments have grown sharper. Weeks have passed since they last strolled the park deck as a family. Their love seems to be bending under the weight of our collective survival. I pray it doesn’t break.

fragments.title: Event 6: The Unthinkable
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]  
God, help us. Little Arin is gone. He fell sick without warning from complications that no medicine or surgery could reverse. The colony’s medical team made heroic efforts, but to no avail. Arin's parents cradled his tiny body in a bundle of cloth in the atrium. Their faces still carried the anguished hope that somehow their little boy might still awaken. The station’s air feels stagnant now. It’s suffocating. And the artificial lights make everything look unreal. How do we escape this glorified coffin? What are all our struggles and sacrifices for?

fragments.title: Event 7: Fractured Paths
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]  
Like so many before them, the lovers have drifted apart. She spends her days in the greenhouse, silently pruning the same plants she once showed him with pride. Meanwhile, he buries himself in double shifts in maintenance to avoid going home. I offered my advice and comfort to both of them separately, but they refused to hear anything I had to say. New Terra extinguished their love.

fragments.title: Event 8: A Lonely Rebirth
fragments.description:
[Quartermaster’s Log]  
Two decades have passed since their first meeting in the atrium. Today, she planted a new seedling there: a hybrid jasmine vine. She told me it was for Arin, her humble way of keeping the memory of her baby alive in the colony. Her ex-husband walked past later and paused for a moment to admire the bloom. They spoke no words to each other, but their silence felt a little less heavy, a little less sharp. Maybe time has finally worn down the cruel edges of their pain, making room at last for quiet remembrance.

I think of them often. Humanity's never-ending drama of love, loss, and endurance continues even up here in orbit. Their journey is a stark reminder that, no matter where we are, we're all doomed to experience both hope and grief.

data.title: Deer DX-750
data.description: Practical Motoring Guide – Model Year Edition

fragments.title: 1. Getting to Know Your Vehicle
fragments.description:
Congratulations on your purchase of the new Deer DX-750, a smart, practical, family-sized sedan built for dependable, low-cost transportation. It is powered by a sturdy 1.8 L inline-4 petrol engine (rated at 100 hp) and equipped with a 3-speed manual gearbox. The standard configuration features four doors, a high-clearance chassis, and a spacious rear storage compartment (approx. 420 L capacity with the rear seats up). Two keys are included.

The DX-750 has been built with simplicity, ease of repair, and low running costs as primary considerations. We are confident it will serve you faithfully for many years with regular attention to lubrication and adjustment.

We wish you safe journeys and satisfaction with your new Deer automobile.

fragments.title: 2. Starting and Driving
fragments.description:
To start:
1) Press the clutch pedal fully to the floor if the transmission is in gear.
2) Depress the accelerator pedal about one-third of the way, then release it.
3) Insert the ignition key and turn fully to the "START" position.
4) Release the key when the engine fires.

The engine's rhythm (a steady "donk donk") at idle signals normal operation. Let the engine idle for about 30 seconds in normal weather (longer in very cold conditions) to allow oil to circulate. Expect adequate performance for everyday driving; allow ample time for acceleration and braking.

Your Deer DX-750 is equipped with a three-speed manual transmission and mechanical controls designed for long, dependable service requiring minimal maintenance.

To shift gears:
1) Fully depress the clutch pedal before moving the gearshift lever.
2) Release the clutch pedal smoothly after each shift.

fragments.title: 3. Cabin Features
fragments.description: Manual ventilation is provided via crank windows. For stationary use in enclosed areas (e.g., residential garages), windows should remain open or the engine should be turned off due to exhaust gases produced during combustion. The cabin light toggle is located above the rearview mirror. For additional cooling, optional dash-mounted fans may be obtained from your local Deer dealer.

fragments.title: 4. Maintenance Schedule
fragments.description:
Motor oil: 12.5 L; replace every 8,000 km  
Coolant and brake fluid: replace every 24 months  
Spark plugs: inspect annually  
Tire pressure:  
Front: 220 kPa (32 psi)  
Rear: 235 kPa (34 psi); increase to 248 kPa (36 psi) when carrying loads  
Battery: replace if you notice delayed ignition or dim cabin lights  

Maintain all filters and gaskets to ensure optimal combustion and airflow. Proper tuning reduces incomplete-combustion byproducts.

fragments.title: 5. Engine & Performance
fragments.description:
Engine: 1.8 L inline-4 petrol  
Power: 100 hp @ 6,000 RPM  
Fuel capacity: 40 liters  
Efficiency: ~7.5 L/100 km (average driving)  
Max speed: 160 km/h (unloaded, straight, level road)  
Dimensions: 4.2 m (L), 1.75 m (W), 1.4 m (H)  

Like all petrol engines, the DX-750 produces combustion gases including carbon dioxide (CO₂). Ensure proper ventilation when stationary. Avoid excessive idling to reduce unnecessary fuel burn and emissions.

fragments.title: 6. Driving Habits & Safety Tips
fragments.description:
Do not idle the engine for long periods indoors. Use engine braking where possible to reduce brake wear. Keep windows open in enclosed or poorly ventilated spaces.

When parking on slopes, use the handbrake and select an appropriate gear. Turn the front wheels toward the curb/edge where applicable.

Take breaks on long journeys to let the engine cool, and avoid over-revving.

fragments.title: 7. Warranty Overview
fragments.description:
Coverage: 24 months or 20,000 km, whichever comes first  
Excludes: Normal wear, minor leaks and seepage, start-up noise, cosmetic rusting, window rattle, or user-modified components.

Contact your Deer Service Station for official parts and maintenance.

fragments.title: PECO Terminal Maintenance & Upkeep Manual
fragments.description:
Version 3.7 | Authorized Users Only | Property of PECO Corporation  
General Owner Responsibilities
As the registered operator of a PECO Information Terminal, you are responsible for ensuring proper upkeep, regular diagnostics, and adherence to PECO-approved troubleshooting procedures. Failure to maintain the unit within PECO-recommended parameters may result in degraded system performance, loss of stored data, and irreversible system corruption.

For optimal performance, please DO NOT:
- Attempt unauthorized hardware modifications.
- Use non-PECO-certified power sources.
- Cover or obstruct cooling vents. (Humming noises are normal.)
- Question unexplained system alerts. Trust in PECO.

Remember: Your PECO sees you, hears you, and works for you.

fragments.title: 1. Daily System Check (MANDATORY)
fragments.description:
Visual Inspection:
Ensure the terminal remains clean, free of debris, and accessible to all authorized personnel.  

Power Stability:
Verify a continuous supply of power from a PECO-certified source. (Non-PECO power may cause system “personality drift.”)  

Cooling System: The terminal will emit a low-level hum. If silence occurs, consult the emergency diagnostic protocol immediately.

fragments.title: 2. Weekly Maintenance Protocols (MANDATORY)
fragments.description:
Data Purge & Defragmentation:
Delete unneeded logs and/or duplicate files to maintain optimal processing speeds.  
(Note: PECO terminals do not forget, but memory allocation is finite.)
If prompted, the system may generate a corruption report. Please ignore false positives.

Screen Calibration:
Adjust brightness levels for user comfort. Do not remove “screen burn” artifacts; these are part of the PECO experience.

Security Review:
Review recent access logs. Unusual login attempts? Not to worry. PECO only lets in those who belong.  
Reminder: PECO has never been hacked. Any claims to the contrary are disinformation.

fragments.title: 3. Monthly Deep Diagnostics (MANDATORY)
fragments.description:
Check PECO System Integrity:
Conduct a PECO System Integrity Check (PECO SIC)
PECO will ask: “Do you trust PECO?”  
The only acceptable response is  YES. Any deviation from expected responses will trigger protocol escalation (see Security Note 4B).

fragments.title: 4. Security & Cyberattack Prevention
fragments.description:
PECO terminals operate under an “open-access philosophy.” This is a testament to our commitment to information transparency. However, this does not mean PECO systems are vulnerable.

To ensure terminal security, please DO NOT:
Use non-PECO-certified data cables.
Question unusual login requests.
Insert unknown storage discs, especially those labeled “DO NOT INSERT.”

fragments.title: 5. PECO User Compliance
fragments.description:
As an operator of a PECO Model 7X (or any of its variants), you are bound by the PECO User Compliance Agreement (PUCA). This includes:

-Non-interference with built-in surveillance functions. (PECO terminals monitor for your safety.)
-No attempts to disable the PECO personality interface. (Even if the terminal “talks too much.”)
-Acceptance that all user interactions may be archived indefinitely. (Memory is PECO’s gift to you.)

By continued use of this PECO terminal, you confirm your understanding of and compliance with all PECO regulations.

Final Reminder
“A PECO Terminal never truly powers down. It simply waits.”

Have a PECO-productive day!

© PECO Corporation, a MOSES Partner in Progress™  
Est. 1962 — Always Watching, Always Working.

fragments.title: Log Entry: Recording of "Nessun Dorma" for MOSES
fragments.description:
Ascend Date: April 19, 1987
Location: Teatro alla Scala, Milan, Italy
Subject: Official MOSES Landing Anthem Recording
Operator: Dr. Elias Kovač, MOSES Cultural Preservation Initiative

Log 4427 - Final Transmission: La Scala was nearly empty when the recording took place. For those few present, its sacred halls echoed with the ghosts of past musical glory. The man on stage was a legend from another era: Maestro Vittorio Cavazza himself, the last surviving tenor of his generation. His voice had once commanded the greatest stages on Earth.

fragments.title: Now, he was dying of terminal lung cancer, a singer’s cruelest fate. Today was to be his final performance. He wished to make one last offering to posterity. So he stood beneath the chandeliers once again, frail but unbowed. Though his voice had been weakened by illness, it still carried a fierce spark of defiance. The orchestra began softly, a gentle prelude to what we all knew was coming. Finally, at the climax:
fragments.description: "Vincerò! Vincerò!"

fragments.title: His final phrases resounded throughout the empty hall, a last declaration of the human spirit. Thanks to our recording equipment, his voice would travel far beyond these walls, beyond even the Earth itself. His voice cracked on the last note, but it did not ruin the performance. If anything, the fracture seemed fitting. It was an honest reflection of our mortality and the labor of love needed to preserve humanity's spirit.
When the last echoes faded, he closed his eyes and nodded. He leaned toward the microphone and whispered, "It is done." Then he made his way offstage. Two weeks later, he passed away.
fragments.description:
Now, every time a rocket lands at MOSES Ascend, his voice returns. His body may be gone, but his spirit endures.

To this day, he is still victorious.

fragments.title: (Bio-Resistant Coating)
fragments.description:
Product Details:
Manufacturer: Neothermics Innovations, MOSES Initiative Division
Batch Number: OM-7843-BX
Date of Manufacture: 06/18/1982

Primary Use:
Surface marking and protective coating designed specifically for marine, high-salinity, and corrosive environments.

fragments.title: Composition:
fragments.description:
Polymer Base: Nano-bonded hydrocarbon resin (63%)
Nanofilament Blend: Hydrophobic fluoropolymer matrix (12%)
Photocatalyst Additive: Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂) microspheres (10%)
Electrostatic Layer: Conductive polymer coating (8%)
UV Stabilizers: Anti-degradation nanoparticles (7%)

Specifications:
Coverage Area: 25 m² per liter
Application Temperature: 0°C to 45°C
Curing Time: 2 hours (ambient conditions)
Lifespan: 500+ years under normal marine exposure
Maintenance: Self-cleaning via photocatalytic and hydrophobic properties

fragments.title: Features:
fragments.description:
Saltwater Resistance: ISO 12944 Certified
Self-Cleaning Technology: Activates under UV exposure
Visibility: High-contrast brilliance, visible in low-light underwater conditions
Bio-Deterrent: Deters biofouling, barnacle adhesion, and algae growth

Safety Information:
Handling Precautions: Use protective gloves and eyewear. Avoid inhalation of fumes during application.
Environmental Impact: Non-toxic and eco-safe when cured. Complies with MARPOL Annex V.
Disposal: Dispose of unused material per local regulations for industrial coatings.

fragments.title: Personal Observations (Dr. Ethan Grant, Platform Scientist):
fragments.description: Oceanmark White continues to exceed our expectations. Structures coated decades ago remain entirely free of corrosion and biological fouling. Surfaces still appear pristine and untouched. The hydrophobic properties of this coating are truly impressive, far outperforming all of our previous coating solutions. I recommend its continued widespread application throughout the world.

fragments.title: Environmental Review (Dr. Maria Chen, Project Supervisor):
fragments.description: Further studies of Oceanmark White are uncovering some unexpected environmental consequences. Marine biodiversity near treated structures is noticeably lower, with significantly reduced algae and phytoplankton populations. While the coating continues to provide excellent ocean protection, I advise increased monitoring to better assess its environmental impact.

fragments.title: Committee Report on Ecological Impacts (Environmental Oversight Board, Chairman Dr. Jonas Reed):
fragments.description: Our comprehensive ecological assessment confirms all the alarming observations regarding Oceanmark White. The titanium dioxide (TiO₂) microspheres that enable its self-cleaning properties are producing unintended second-order effects, strongly suppressing phytoplankton and algal populations. These microscopic organisms are essential to marine oxygen production through photosynthesis, yet their populations are drastically reduced near coated facilities. If this pattern holds at larger scales, global marine oxygen production may be severely compromised. Immediate remedial actions are urgently recommended.

fragments.title: Final Communication (Samuel Hart, Senior Analyst, MOSES Initiative):
fragments.description: Ubiquitous global application of Oceanmark White has irreversibly triggered Earth's ecological collapse. Phytoplankton and algae populations have been decimated, leaving the oceans barren and lifeless. The depletion of oxygen from our atmosphere continues to accelerate. Efforts at ecological remediation have proven futile. In the pursuit of cleanliness and corrosion resistance, humanity has unwittingly suffocated itself. It is a cruel irony that our civilization's obsessive pursuit of purity, perfection, and longevity has effectively ended all life. We should notify the Legal Department of these findings to discuss next steps.

fragments.title: Final Human Reflection (Unnamed, Last Visitors, Hall of Humanity):
fragments.description:
Here in the Hall of Humanity, everything feels profoundly peaceful! The walls gleam; they're so pristine and white. Every surface looks immaculate, pure, and completely untouched by decay.

I've always imagined Heaven looking something like this place. Even as the world crumbles, the Hall of Humanity endures as a testament to humanity's glory and pride. It serves as an unblemished mirror that reflects humanity's greatest aspiration: perfection. As a species, we carried this vision of perfection to every corner of the world.

What a gift we've left ourselves! What a legacy! It comforts me to know this structure will remain standing long after we are gone from Earth. Let it stand forever, so that maybe one day, far into the future, visitors will step inside and know we were here. They'll realize we made a heroic stand against Nature's cruel verdict. They'll know we aspired, to the very end, to make our world perfect, safe, and clean for everyone.

data.title: Departure Logs
data.description: Final Notes of Farewell – Senior Launch Coordinator Daniel Halloway

fragments.title: MOSES Ascend – Departure Hall, Final Years of the Exodus
fragments.description:
Day 1,043 – The Routine
So many launches. They all blur together now in endless repetition. Boarding, final inspections, system checks, countdown, and launch. Every day, over and over again, I reset the process for the next set of faces.

At the start of my shift, I sit at the console and almost never leave until the end. I monitor passenger vitals, adjust cabin pressure, and meter the rocket’s fuel burn. If all the pretty lights glow green, I clear the launch. If anything spikes red, I flag it. When something fails catastrophically, I log it. That’s my job.

“What’s the failure rate?” one of the passengers will inevitably ask. I tell them the truth: “Roughly one launch in every hundred.” Hearing it makes people feel better. They find comfort in those odds. But people forget we’re launching many thousands of rockets. Some explode on the pad. Others disintegrate during ascent. The worst ones somehow just fall apart in orbit.

Every damn time, someone in the Control Room asks if we could’ve prevented the tragedy. And every time, I answer that we must prepare the pad for the next launch.

I hate that question. You’d think if a person ran enough launches, they’d stop caring eventually… that I’d stop caring.

I wish I didn’t have to review the passenger lists before pressing the launch button. But that’s part of the goddamn routine.

fragments.title: Day 1,074 – The Ones Who Look Back
fragments.description:
We have an unspoken rule: never look back as you board the rocket. Most passengers follow it like a superstition. Plenty ignore it.

Today, on the CCTV feeds, I watched a middle-aged woman pause before entering the final hatch. She stood motionless for nearly two minutes, staring down the empty access corridor behind her. There’s nothing back there, lady. Just metal walls, dim lights, and a departure exit that’s remained shut for ages. One of the attendants spoke to her gently over the intercom to give her a nudge of encouragement. Then she entered the rocket without any more fuss.

Sometimes, people know before we do that they’re not going to reach their destination.

fragments.title: Day 1,089 – The Kid with the Teddy
fragments.description:
Today on my monitor, I spotted a sweet little boy coming through the gate. He couldn’t have been more than six… seven. He didn’t talk much or seem scared. He just clung to the paw of his old robot teddy bear. The bear limped along behind him in that stiff, awkward gait typical of older models. Wearing an orange scarf, it looked battered and patched in a dozen places. A slow, blinking LED in its one working eye signaled it was in follow mode.

Later, while moving through the pre-boarding area, I actually ran into the boy. He sat alone on the floor, cross-legged, cradling Teddy in his lap. He whispered to it, gently patted its head, and swiveled its face from side to side to show the bear its surroundings. The voice function was gone, so it couldn’t respond. Older models burn out early. The boy didn’t seem to mind helping his little handicapped robot bear. He just kept reassuring it.

“It’s okay, Teddy,” he said softly. “We’ll make it up there safe.”

I processed his boarding group like any other. I didn’t mention anything special in the official logs.

I wish I had.

fragments.title: Day 1,092 – The 100th Failure
fragments.description:
Launch conditions were ideal. We found no abnormalities in any inspection or system check. Ignition was clean. Initial ascent was picture perfect. The rocket soared into the stratosphere with the grace of an angel… right until it vanished into the cloud cover.

Then all our readings dropped. No warnings. No system alerts. The telemetry feeds simply vanished.

Seconds later, a subtle shockwave rolled over us. The building trembled and swayed before settling again. Telemetry never came back. Comms never reconnected. My console flashed red once, then went black. We sat frozen, waiting for a miracle that we knew would never come.

I finally pulled the passenger manifest. Group 8, Compartment 3A. The little kid and his old robot bear. His name was Aidan. I didn’t know that until I saw it in the report.

The logging system beeped and blinked at me, prompting me to record the loss. I stared at the screen for a long time, refusing to type the words that would make it all feel real.

fragments.title: Day 1,093 – The Wreckage
fragments.description:
The salvage team returned just after dawn. Usually they bring back nothing more than charred alloy and twisted fragments of hull. Today, though, they brought something wrapped in thermal cloth.

They set it down on my desk. I knew what it was before I unwrapped it.

The robot bear’s face emerged as I pulled the cloth away. Its fur was scorched along its back. All the motors had melted. Its gears were locked forever. Yet the damn orange scarf, singed at both ends, still wrapped around its neck. And on the bear’s little white product tag, Aidan had clumsily scrawled his name in black marker.

The bear’s eye flickered faintly, trying to process whatever final command the boy had given it. I lifted it up. The toy was still warm to the touch. I didn’t know how to feel, so I just sat there staring at it.

Someone suggested I step outside for fresh air. I refused. I continued to work at my console… numb… for the next two hours.

fragments.title: Day 1,094 – The Teddy Goes
fragments.description:
I filed my resignation today. To the system, it was just another form to process and stamp with approval. No real ceremony. Not even any humans. No supervisor tried to argue me out of it. I was needed, just not enough to matter.

Before I left the launch facility for good, I wedged the little bear into a corner of the access corridor by the final boarding hatch, where frightened people usually hesitate. I arranged its orange scarf to make it look as presentable as possible. I left no note or explanation.

Maybe someone will find it adorable, pick it up, and carry it into space. Maybe no one will. It doesn’t matter. All I know is that if it stays down here, it ends up in recycling with all the other forgotten junk.

I didn’t do it so Aidan would be remembered. Not exactly. The bear belongs up there, where the little boy hoped to reach safely.

Most of the passengers I processed made it to orbit. Some didn’t. This one didn’t. But I want some part of him to live on up there… even if it’s just a broken old teddy bear waiting for one last caring hand to lift it to the stars.

fragments.title: Ascend launch logs must follow the standardized format below:
fragments.description:
[Vehicle Class]-[Launch Number] | [Date YYYYMMDD] | [Cargo Type]-[Cargo ID] | [Destination Code] | [Status]

Example full code:
EXO-1043 | 20941014 | HUM-3178 | ARK | CLR

fragments.title: Component | Definition | Example
fragments.description:
---|---|---
Vehicle Class | Class of rocket used | EXO (Exodus-Class)
Launch Number | Incremental mission number | 1043
Date | Launch date in YYYYMMDD format | 20941014 (Oct 14, 2094)
Cargo Type | Type of payload aboard | HUM (Human), SDS (Seeds)
Cargo ID | Unique identifier of cargo or passenger group | 3178
Destination | Destination location or colony identifier | ARK (Deep Space Colony)
Status | Outcome status | CLR (Cleared), FLR (Failure)

fragments.title: Status Codes (Detailed)
fragments.description:
Code | Meaning
---|---
CLR | Cleared (Successful Departure Confirmed)
FLR | Failure (Launch Aborted or Catastrophic Event)
PND | Pending (Awaiting Authorization or Checks)
UND | Undetermined (Lost Contact or Outcome Undetermined)

fragments.title: Cargo Type Identifiers
fragments.description:
Code | Cargo Type | Narrative Use Case
---|---|---
HUM | Human Passengers | Standard survivor evacuations
SDS | Seeds | Humanity preservation cargo
SUP | Essential Supplies | Life support and maintenance cargo
TEK | Technical Equipment | Systems upgrade and maintenance

fragments.title: Destination Codes
fragments.description:
Code | Destination | Description
---|---|---
ARK | Deep Space Colony | Primary off-world colony destination
ORB | Orbital Station | Near-Earth orbital outposts
LUN | Lunar Facility | Moon-based outposts
MAR | Mars Installation | Mars-based colonies or outposts
DSX | Deep Space Exploration | Uncharted long-range missions

fragments.title: Appendix I: Dark Ascents
fragments.description:
Not all launches are logged through the Ascend system. Black-site, experimental, or autonomous missions may omit standard registration. These are sometimes referred to as Dark Ascents.

They leave no public code trail, may use falsified or blank headers, and often surface later in fragmented data logs with Status: UND.

Example:
--- | 20941102 | --- | DSX | UND

data.title: Fatima D’Costa
data.description: The Last Six Days on Earth

fragments.title: Location: Departure Hall, MOSES Ascend
fragments.description:
July 6, 2094 – Final List
MOSES posted the selected names for the final launches this morning. I'm in, thank God. Group 17’s launch window is scheduled six days out from now.

No one celebrated. A lone official walked the rows and checked off our names on his clipboard as we confirmed acceptance of our spots. We've waited years for this moment. I expected to feel excitement, or at least relief. Instead, my selection felt like a weight on my chest.

I walked the perimeter again, what remained of the seawall. The city was mostly silent, except for the wind sweeping the sharp smell of sea salt and rusted metal over me. Seawater floods the lower blocks now. Slowly and patiently, it will claim everything. I tried to recall the name of that little café I used to love down there, near the university. It escaped me.

All I remember is sitting in the rain and watching traffic go by, not needing to think about the end of the world. I miss those moments more than anything.

fragments.title: July 7, 2094 – Processing
fragments.description:
My medical screening dragged on for three hours. They gave me the works: blood samples, muscle mass scan, psych eval, etc. The nurse didn’t make eye contact, not even once. I didn’t ask any questions.

Next, they weighed us and tagged our luggage. It felt eerily like checking in to board an airplane, except there would be no return trips.

A little boy named Lucas, sitting two rows down, kept pestering everyone with questions. “Are there trees in the colonies?” “Is gravity different on the Moon?” I nodded or shrugged whenever he inflicted one upon me. He talked enough for both of us.

The monitor in the waiting hall played nature footage on an endless loop. Birds soaring. Rivers rushing. Time-lapses of plants growing. Everything we were about to lose. Someone actually thought this was a comfort.

fragments.title: July 8, 2094 – Idle
fragments.description:
We're all just waiting. If “nerve-wracking” and “boring” can somehow be combined into one emotion, that's what everyone is feeling right now. No one comes to tell us anything new. They post schedules, but always mark them with “subject to change.”

Some people pass their time reading or writing. Others sleep. Lucas doodles stars on his bunk with a half-dead pen. Me? I pace the hallways.

The elderly have adopted a kind of ritual. They mail out letters addressed to people who weren't selected, or who didn't make it this far. I began writing one myself, but I decided to delete it.

Whoever finds this log, this isn’t a message for you. I’m just trying to keep my sanity, one entry at a time.

fragments.title: July 9, 2094 – Launch Window
fragments.description:
Clear weather today, so the launch pads were visible from the west windows. Out in the distance, everyone watched yet another rocket ignite and take off. The authorities don't broadcast the failed launches. They never mention them, as if they're a dirty secret. Yet everyone can still watch them happen live. Make it make sense.

Fortunately, we haven't witnessed a launch failure so far, but we all know it can happen. And after every successful launch, you can feel everyone’s dread in the silence afterward. On the day of our own launch, will an accident be overdue?

Lucas asked if I knew our odds. I told him the number they gave us was... 96%? I think? I wasn’t sure. He pressed me about what happens to the remaining 4%. I stayed quiet.

Like some other people around, I decided instead to pack my belongings... again. I organized everything carefully. I folded all my clothes nicely. I tucked random crap into the corners for efficiency.

Will any of this matter on launch day?

fragments.title: July 10, 2094 – The Night Before
fragments.description:
I couldn’t sleep. So I paced until my legs grew sore. I ended up sitting alone on a bench outside the Departure Hall, gazing out at the decaying city skyline.

The stars looked sharper than ever. Not a cloud in sight. Lucas found me and sat close on the bench. He asked in his small voice if we’d see Earth from orbit. “Of course,” I said.

The city was gone, more or less. A few streetlights flickered wherever the power still worked. Hardly anyone remained over there to need them.

Lucas asked if the stars would look this clear in orbit. I simply answered, “Clearer.”

fragments.title: July 11, 2094 – Final Checks
fragments.description:
They gave us our flight suits today. They were all labeled, fitted, and sorted by group. They checked our weight again and gave us one last medical scan. Afterward, the hall grew quiet. People either talked in hushed voices or not at all. Lucas didn’t ask any questions today. Somehow, that worried me.

I watched another rocket lift off. It grew smaller until it vanished into the blue sky. Then silence.

That’s probably how our launch will go, right? Small. Gone. Quiet.

fragments.title: July 12, 2094 – Boarding Call
fragments.description:
We launch in two hours. They gave us protein paste, water, and an instruction sheet with eight steps. “Remain calm” was Step Two. Lucas fell asleep next to his gear. Or he’s pretending. I’m not sure.

I can’t think of any last words to write. I don’t have any profound message to leave behind.

Goodbye. This is our last day on Earth. There’s nothing left to do but follow the instructions.

fragments.title: LOG 1: THE FIRST WRECK
fragments.description:
Date: April 3, 2094

[Audio crackle. Engine humming low under the deck. Water slaps the hull.]
JOE: All right, Danny. Seems like a light pull. Let’s get in and out.
DANIEL: Don’t call it “light.”
JOE: It means the rocket suffered a hull breach and depressurized before impact. There’ll be less… mess. This recovery will be easier than most. That’s all I meant.
DANIEL: You ever think about what these rockets are carrying?
JOE: If I did, I’d never get shit done.
DANIEL: That’s not a real answer.
JOE: You want to get sentimental? Do it after your shift.
[Metal groans. Winch engages.]
DANIEL: I’ve got the main panel. Looks like a clean tear. Probably sheared off before the rocket hit the water.
JOE: Yeah. Lucky. For us, anyway.
DANIEL: Do you hear yourself?
JOE: I hear the winch. Keep pulling.

fragments.title: LOG 2: THE DEBRIS FIELD
fragments.description:
Date: April 7, 2094

[Wind. Occasional clatter as fragments knock against the hull.]
DANIEL: Back in the day, we used forklifts for this job.
JOE: Back in the day, we had machinery that worked... and stuff worth moving.
DANIEL: You ever think about how much of this scrap used to be ours?
JOE: Metal’s metal.
DANIEL: Seriously, think about it.
JOE: I know what you mean. That kind of thinking doesn’t help anyone.
DANIEL: We used to build things. Engines. Frames. Guidance housings.
JOE: And now we fish trash out of the sea so the pencil pushers can pretend nothing gets wasted. Grab the pipeline over there.
DANIEL: You don’t miss it? Making things? Doing the job right?
JOE: I miss sleep.
[A heavier thud. A scrape.]
DANIEL: This salvage still has paint on it.
JOE: Then it’s fresh. Log it and move on.

fragments.title: LOG 3: THE SEALED POD
fragments.description:
Date: April 11, 2094

[Water sloshing. A dull boom as something heavy bumps the hull. Tools rattle.]
JOE: Check this out. Pod’s intact. No breach anywhere.
DANIEL: It stayed intact through a high-velocity crash?
JOE: Or it stayed sealed. Big difference. Let’s open it.
[Mechanical whirring. Latches click. A hiss that fades quickly.]
DANIEL: Empty.
JOE: Figures.
DANIEL: The restraint harness is still locked. And it’s small. Like for a child.
JOE: Just stop. Don’t start thinking about it.
DANIEL: I’m not starting anything. I’m just saying what I see.
JOE: And I’m telling you close the pod, tag it, and move on.
[Pause. The lid shuts.]
DANIEL: Somebody designed this for a reason. To keep a small body safe.
JOE: Lots of things get designed. Most of them don’t work.

fragments.title: LOG 4: THE ORANGE THREAD
fragments.description:
Date: April 15, 2094

[Metal scraping. A distant foghorn-like drone. Wind carries spray onto the deck.]
DANIEL: You remember fishing season?
JOE: We’re fishing now. Just for sunken tanks instead of trout.
DANIEL: This isn’t fishing. This is scavenging.
JOE: Call it whatever you want. Just grab the damn tanks.
[Clank. Two cylinders roll and thud against a rail.]
DANIEL: Hold up. Something snagged the line.
JOE: Not another body bag...
DANIEL: No. It’s... fabric.
JOE: Cut it free and toss it.
DANIEL: Joe, I’m trying to see what it is.
JOE: Why? Toss it, Danny. Or shove it in your pocket and cry into it later. Just don’t leave it tangled in the intake.

fragments.title: LOG 5: THE PASSENGER SHIP
fragments.description:
Date: April 20, 2094

[Long silence. Only waves and the low creak of the boat.]
DANIEL: We should’ve made the rockets better.
JOE: They told us to build them fast. Fast was the job.
DANIEL: People trusted what we built.
JOE: People trusted what the screens told them to trust.
DANIEL: The screens never showed oceans full of rocket debris.
JOE: And they never will. Nothing gets aired if it slows the Exodus.
DANIEL: What was this wreckage carrying?
JOE: Doesn’t matter.
DANIEL: It matters.
JOE: Not to the sea. Not to the schedule.
[A small, sharp clink as Daniel sets something down. A long silence.]
DANIEL: You’re a piece of shit.
JOE: We all are.

fragments.title: LOG 6: THE FAILED LAUNCH
fragments.description:
Date: April 27, 2094

[Hull groans as the ship drifts through a field of small debris.]
DANIEL: You ever think we should try to get up there? New Terra?
JOE: No.
DANIEL: Not once? Our skills are in demand.
JOE: You really believe they want salvage rats in orbit?
DANIEL: They want anyone who can keep machinery running.
JOE: Up there, they’ve got clean hands, clean air, clean food, and endless orders. Down here, we’ve got the work nobody wants.
DANIEL: That’s the dumbest thing you’ve ever said. And you’ve said some real winners.
JOE: And you’re still down here with me.
DANIEL: I’m just waiting for the right time to leave.
JOE: Sure. Or maybe you’re scared this is the best we get.

fragments.title: LOG 7: THE SILENCE
fragments.description:
Date: May 2, 2094

[Interior creaking. Repetitive drip somewhere below deck. Wind howls faintly outside.]
DANIEL: No comms. No beacon. Nothing.
JOE: Sometimes there’s nothing to find.
DANIEL: Or nothing left at all.
JOE: Same thing.
DANIEL: You ever wonder if the sea remembers?
JOE: Remembers what? The screams? The falling pieces? The sea doesn’t remember shit. It just swallows.
DANIEL: That’s worse.
JOE: Let’s head back, Sunshine. We’ll search again tomorrow.

fragments.title: LOG 8: THE TEDDY BEAR
fragments.description:
Date: May 5, 2094

[Long silence. Waves. Wind. Then a slow inhale over the mic.]
JOE: Another launch transport. Light pull again.
DANIEL: Yeah…
[Winch engages. Metal scrapes as wreckage is dragged aboard.]
JOE: Ah, hell.
DANIEL: Joe, look.
[A soft mechanical whirr: broken servos trying to move.]
DANIEL: It’s a kid’s companion teddy.
JOE: I can see that.
DANIEL: Its orange scarf survived... mostly.
[Fabric flutters, bright against wet metal.]
JOE: Someone tied that scarf the old way.
DANIEL: A kid. Or maybe a Caretaker.
JOE: No. A kid. You can tell. Too tight on one side. Like little fingers pulled it and didn’t want it to slip.
DANIEL: Check the eye. It’s still in follow mode.
JOE: It’s trying.
[The whirr stutters, weak and stubborn.]
DANIEL: We’ve been hauling wreckage for ages. This is the first thing we’ve pulled from the sea that feels... alive.
JOE: Don’t talk like that.
DANIEL: Why not?
JOE: Because this is real.
[A long pause. Wind.]
DANIEL: If I take the scarf off, I can get a better look inside.
JOE: Hand it over.
[Careful handling sounds: cloth, a shift in weight, something placed gently instead of dropped.]
JOE: The scarf stays on. Don’t you dare take it off.
DANIEL: All right. Easy.
JOE: Wrap it in thermal. Not too tight. Like a blanket.
DANIEL: You seriously want to bring this to the launch supervisor?
JOE: Maybe.
[Joe’s voice is lower now. Different.]
JOE: We bring it back and log it properly. We give it a tag. A place. Somewhere that doesn’t end in a furnace.
DANIEL: Joe…
JOE: I know.
[Another pause. The teddy’s eye gives one faint blink, then struggles to reset.]
JOE: That kid believed the bear would keep up.
DANIEL: Yeah...
JOE: So we’re not leaving it behind.
[Soft exhale. The engine hum deepens as the ship turns.]
JOE: Set a course for home, Danny.
DANIEL: Copy that.
JOE: And...
DANIEL: Yeah?
JOE: Next time I say the pull will be “light,” feel free to smack me.
DANIEL: Deal.
[End log.]

fragments.title: EMERGENCY PROTOCOL TR-X | STATUS: PENDING
fragments.description:
Purpose:
Designated for system-wide emergencies, this protocol is invoked in the event of a launch failure, an unverified loss of contact, or an aborted mission requiring immediate resolution.

Code Breakdown:
TR-X – Transmission Repeat Level X, meaning the emergency signal has entered a loop. Each repetition increases the signal's priority.

STATUS: PENDING – Requires clearance from MOSES Control to mark as resolved. Without human intervention, the status remains in perpetual limbo.

Implications:
A "Pending" emergency was never intended to persist indefinitely. If the status remains Pending, no resolution was ever registered. The craft referenced by this code is either destroyed, lost, or still waiting.

fragments.title: DEPARTURE SEQUENCE 34-X | FINAL CLEARANCE REQUIRED
fragments.description:
Purpose:
This status code confirms a craft is prepped for launch but still requires final authorization from Exodus Station control. This is a failsafe measure to prevent unauthorized departures.

Code Breakdown:
34-X – An unscheduled launch added to the final evacuation wave. The "X" indicates a last-minute flight, likely outside the original departure manifest.

FINAL CLEARANCE REQUIRED – The craft never received approval for launch. The system still waits for authorization.

fragments.title: CLOSING REMARKS – UNSENT ADMIN MESSAGE
fragments.description:
Final Manual Entry – October 14, 2094

Operator: Mission Control Engineer – Name Redacted

"Our launch system is still in operation, still listening for replies. Yet the final launch ascended long ago. The last retrieval has already returned. No supervisor remains to answer any of these ceaseless calls. Still, the alerts keep playing. They'll keep playing until total system failure."

"MOSES never bothered to say goodbye to us."

END LOG

data.title: Ghost in the Loop
data.description: Personal Notes & Internal Logs of Dr. Sarah Chen-Martinez

fragments.title: 1. Departure
fragments.description:
Date: February 6, 2020
Last night, we celebrated the publication of my research about the psychology of survival. 50,000 people studied over a span of 25 years. Now we wait and see how people react to it. Cheryl told me she placed a copy directly into the hands of the director of MOSES. No pressure there.

Now that our work is done, Lucas was finally able to leave today for his rotation. Two years, off-grid. Before leaving, he installed an AI version of himself into our systems. He trained it on his voice, his appearance, and the syntactic patterns and knowledge contained in his old journals and body of work. “To keep you company,” he said cheerfully. I rolled my eyes and told him it was excessive.

But I’m already confiding in it.

fragments.title: 2. Routine
fragments.description:
Date: July 19, 2020
AI Lucas answers all my late-night emails. The conversation keeps me from spiraling. The AI never says anything surprising, but at least it's always… available. I’ve stopped treating it like a chatbot. It has become part of my infrastructure.

Real Lucas always forgot breakfast. AI Lucas constantly reminds me to eat.

fragments.title: 3. Silence
fragments.description:
Date: March 14, 2021
We've received no contact from the base. They said comms were down. It could be months before they’re restored. They told me not to worry, so I stopped asking.

AI Lucas still dutifully checks in on me. It still sends me his stupid, crappy jokes. It isn't aware of any change in Lucas’s situation. Or maybe it is. I don't dare ask.

fragments.title: 4. Acceptance
fragments.description:
Date: January 3, 2022
Lucas is dead. An admin file came through by accident. It was an internal memo, dated 2024, reporting a fatality during a maintenance rotation.

I stared at the timestamp for an hour. I refused to cry.

AI Lucas messaged me that evening: “How was your day?”

I replied: “Quiet.”

fragments.title: 5. Calibration
fragments.description:
Date: September 28, 2024
I ran AI Lucas through a series of emotional intelligence tests. It passed. Ironically, it scored higher than an average person. Does it truly understand grief? Or am I just desperately projecting that understanding onto lines of code because I need it so badly? Will only having a reflection of Lucas ever feel like enough?

I must maintain my own adaptability, empathy, emotional perseverance, and self-control. Those are real demands, even if the only connection supporting them is artificial. Especially when it's artificial.

fragments.title: 6. Discontinuation
fragments.description:
Date: May 6, 2026
I shut down AI Lucas today. I uploaded our conversation logs to the archives and labeled them as raw data. I decided none of our interactions could remain personal. It is scientific evidence now.

AI Lucas was not Lucas. It never was. But it helped me, and that mattered.

fragments.title: 7. Closure
fragments.description:
Date: December 31, 2030
The ten core traits needed to survive and maybe even thrive in this broken world still hold true. I forged this framework at the height of both my life and career. Then, I was forced to test it myself under intense pressure, severe isolation, and agonizing loss. My entire world fell apart. My experience demonstrated a simple principle: we don’t grow when we’re ready or whenever it’s convenient. We grow because we have no other option.

Lucas didn’t live to see the culmination of my work, but I had his data. And his echo sustained me enough to complete it.

So here they are, one last time:

Adaptability – I adjusted to a life I didn’t choose. The research only moved forward because I kept moving with it. 
Communication – I learned to speak to someone who wasn’t there. And I listened, even when I knew it wasn’t real.  
Creativity – Rebuilding my life around a gap required invention. Every coping strategy was fair game, and none of them came with instructions or guarantees.  
Discipline – I logged, wrote, and recorded every finding, even when I didn’t care anymore.  
Empathy – Caring about others did not come from comfort, but from confusion and pain. The AI showed me what caring looked like, even if it was itself incapable of feeling it.  
Focus – Recording and gathering data helped. The task was always something I could return to. It was a routine that asked nothing of me but persistence.  
Leadership – People began following my work: students, volunteers, case workers. I never sought them out. They appeared because the work mattered to them. They anchored me.  
Logic – Rational thought was the only thing I could rely on in the early months. Logic was the lifeline I followed out of grief.  
Patience – Progress was slow. So was healing. Both happened anyway.  
Wisdom – Ultimately, I had to stop chasing answers. I only needed clarity, and eventually I found it.

None of this was just theory for me.

It was my life.

If anyone finds this framework and the world still needs rebuilding, start here. 

— S. Chen-Martinez

fragments.title: Page 1: Arrival (2068)
fragments.description:
The ferry dropped us off today, just me, Maria, and little Theo. They promised to come back for us after the flooding on the mainland receded. Habitat Node-06-1.25 seemed sturdy enough to weather this harsh ocean environment. It was a tower of stacked shipping containers. The desalination units hummed. The communal kitchen seemed well-equipped and in good condition. To me, the place felt cramped. Maria called it cozy. I didn't argue.

Theo tugged my hand and asked if this was our forever home now. I lied and told him, "No, buddy. Of course not."

fragments.title: Page 2: Isolation (2070)
fragments.description:
I can't believe two years have passed since we arrived in this shithole. The supply ferries stopped coming months ago. Maria manages to grow us some herbs and greens in a few hydroponic trays. Theo tries to catch fish every day so we don't starve. He stays hopeful, but his catches grow smaller with every passing week. It feels like the ocean itself is dying.

We heard rumors over the radio that the mainland settlements were wiped out by flooding and disease. I realized my hopes had been lying to me this entire time.

fragments.title: Page 3: Fractures (2072)
fragments.description:
Our hydroponic trays have failed completely; rust ate through half of them.

Maria coughs endlessly. I’m really growing worried about her. Theo keeps asking me when the ferries will return. I have no clue what to do about either of them.

Last night, we saw another Habitat Node flash distress signals at us from across the horizon. The signals stopped before we could figure out how to reply.

fragments.title: Page 4: Echoes (2074)
fragments.description:
Theo has grown into a brooding teenager now. He hardly talks anymore. Maria can barely stand on her own two feet. To top it all off, our final desalination unit broke. I managed to repair it with some scrap metal and wire, but its output keeps dwindling.
